comments.author: Raghavendra Bagalkoti
comments.comment: The biggest jump is treating agents as state machines, not chat loops. Once tool calls can fail, timeout, or return partial data, explicit state and retries matter more than the model choice. Are you mostly using LangChain for RAG pipelines, or for tool-using agents?

comments.author: Karthik Chakravarthy
comments.comment: The useful split I’ve seen is less “25 RAG types” and more 4 design choices: query rewriting, retrieval strategy, reranking, and answer verification. Most production failures come from the boring middle, bad chunks, weak metadata, no reranker, or no eval set tied to real user questions. Which of these architectures actually improved your eval scores versus just changing the diagram?

comments.author: Puteri Irene Alia Abdul Hadi
comments.comment: The harness shift is real: once the model can observe state, call tools, retry, and keep a durable task log, prompt quality stops being the main bottleneck. The hard part becomes permissions, evals, rollback, and knowing when the agent should stop. What failure mode are you seeing most often in desktop agents: wrong tool use, stale memory, or unsafe persistence?

comments.author: Aurimas Griciūnas
comments.comment: One thing I’d add: MCP standardizes the tool/data boundary, but it doesn’t remove the need for retrieval contracts. For agentic RAG, I’ve found each source needs explicit metadata guarantees, freshness expectations, auth scope, and failure behavior, otherwise the agent just gets more ways to retrieve the wrong context.

comments.author: Pooja Jain
comments.comment: The failure mode I see most is that teams version prompts but not context. Metric definitions, retrieval chunks, schema docs, and examples need the same CI checks as code: freshness, ownership, tests, and rollback. What is the first context artifact you would put under version control, metric definitions or table docs?

comments.author: Fulvio Mascara
comments.comment: The hard part is not launching two agents in parallel, it is preserving enough state for the second agent to make a better decision than a fresh run. I’d want the orchestrator to standardize 4 artifacts per handoff: goal, constraints, diff, and verification result. What does it emit when the two harnesses disagree?

comments.author: abdelrahman Ashraf omar
comments.comment: One retrieval detail that changed how I build RAG: evaluate the retriever separately before judging the final answer. If recall@k is weak, no amount of prompt tuning or reranking will save the system, because the model never sees the right evidence in the first place.

comments.author: Jody Roberts
comments.comment: The underrated part here is separating context between planner, builder, and evaluator. If the same context carries all three roles, the evaluator often grades the story the generator told, not the artifact itself. I’ve found the spec needs executable checks for at least the boring stuff: tests, lint, type checks, diff boundaries. How are you handling failures where the evaluator passes but the product still feels wrong?

comments.author: Ahmed Zafar
comments.comment: The hardest part here is probably not the model accuracy, it is deciding when the system should stay silent. A form coach that fires false corrections at 30 FPS can teach bad habits fast, so I’d be curious how you handle confidence thresholds, occlusion, and “not enough signal” cases in the browser.

comments.author: Vaibhav Sisinty
comments.comment: The real shift is treating the agent like a long-running dev environment, not a prompt box. The highest ROI habit is still boring: check the diff, tighten permissions, then rerun with a smaller context instead of starting over.
